the thing is with with android vs iPhone debate, its really putting apple in a disadvantageous position because though they are comparable in terms of marketshare, at the end of the way, you are still choosing between what one company can offer, vs what many, many different companies can offer. So in that way with this kind of system, its hard for the iPhone to win because it is competing with different android phones, with different strengths and weaknesses, under one umbrella which leads to the android usually coming out onto because some android phones are better, even if the experience on the iPhone is better in most cases. for example with features. yes, android is going to have more features because as a whole, you have many more companies doing many more things. same with customization. You have one OS, for one company, vs a platform which must be a one size fits all situation that can be used by anybody, which automatically means more ability to do things differently. so ofc android will be better in this way ultimately, even if the experience of that customization, is worse.

One thing I want to mention and that's not permanent to this video but there's other videos I've watched here so it would be printed too is original Android phones did not used to have a specific set charging limits. The manufacturers used to just make the wiring Overkill and basically even if you had a phone that had a 1300 milliamp hour battery you could hook it into a 5 volt 2 amp charging brick and it would literally charge your phone at 10 w even though you were only supposed to charge it 4. Ws maybe even 5 w now with the new iPhones and Android phones it's not the charger itself. That is necessarily the limitation. You can buy 100 w charging brick now and plug it in to say like something like a Google Pixel 7 pro and the phone is still only going to draw 25 w instead of actually charging at 100 w now. Or you can take something like a Samsung s24 or s25 ultra that can charge at 45 w and hook it into a charging brick. That's only good for 25 w and then the charging brick is the limitation and not the device. Whereas in the early days of the Android ecosystem, the charging brick was strictly the charging limit. If you managed to get your hands on a 2.4 amp charging brick with a thick enough charging cable, your phone would literally charge at 12 w and back in the early days of the Android phones some Android phones only came with a 4-watt charger in the box because their battery was so small and typically the charger itself was paired with the phone because it would take around an hour and a half with not using your phone to charge your phone's battery from completely dead to full. But back then if you plugged your phone in and charged it to 100%, it would typically last you all day even with using it. So worrying about battery life wasn't as big of a deal. Some phones would even go multiple days on a single charge that were Androids. Nowadays that is not necessarily the case and now we care about how big and how fast the charger is considering that some phones nowadays have a battery that is 4.2 volts nominal with a 6,000 milliamp cell versus the pretty average standard 3.7 volt cell at 1,500 milliamps back in the early days of Android. So the battery capacity is nine times bigger in some smartphones today in 2025 than it was in your standard smartphone in 2010 and yet that larger battery capacity is still only a 2-day battery capacity cuz many smartphones in 2010 were shooting for a 2-day battery life. Sometimes even a 3-day battery life on a single charge.

When it comes to choosing between an iPhone and an Android phone, the decision often boils down to what you prioritize as a user. Here’s a breakdown for each platform:

Why Choose an iPhone?
	1.	Global Warranty: Apple is one of the few brands offering a worldwide warranty. If you’re a frequent traveler or someone who values reliable after-sales support globally, the iPhone is a clear winner.
	2.	Balanced Experience: While iPhones may not dominate in any single area, they offer solid performance across the board—design, camera, software, and more. Critics might argue about camera quality, but trust me, photos taken on an iPhone never feel “cheap” or subpar.
	3.	Ease of Use: Apple’s iOS is designed with simplicity in mind. Whether you’re a tech novice or an experienced user, the seamless and intuitive interface makes it incredibly user-friendly.
	4.	App Optimization: Developers often prioritize iOS, resulting in apps that are more polished and perform better on iPhones.
	5.	Longevity: With years of regular software updates, iPhones maintain their relevance and performance longer than most Android devices.
	6.	Resale Value: iPhones consistently hold their value better than Android phones, making them a smarter financial choice if you plan to upgrade later.

Why Choose Android?
	1.	Best-in-Class Features: If you’re looking for specific standout features like the best smartphone camera, high zoom capabilities, or cutting-edge low-light performance Android phones like the Pixel or Galaxy S series are the way to go.
	2.	Customization: Android is perfect for users who enjoy tweaking their device to suit their personal preferences. From themes to widgets and custom default apps, Android offers unmatched flexibility.
	3.	Wide Range of Options: Android’s ecosystem includes devices at every price point, from budget-friendly phones to ultra-premium flagships, ensuring there’s something for everyone.
	4.	Hardware Innovation: From foldable phones to high-refresh-rate screens, massive batteries, and blazing-fast charging, Android brands are constantly pushing the boundaries of what a smartphone can do.
	5.	Expandable Storage: Many Android devices still offer expandable storage via microSD cards, a feature that Apple has never included.
	6.	Diverse Form Factors: Whether you want a rugged phone, a gaming phone, or the latest in futuristic design, Android has a device tailored to your needs.

Final Verdict
If you want a phone that “just works,” delivers consistency, long-term software support, and better resale value, the iPhone is your best bet. On the other hand, if you value innovation, customization, or specialized features, Android offers a level of versatility that iPhones simply can’t match. At the end of the day, it’s all about what you need and what you value most in a smartphone.

My take:
I decided for my last phone to try an Android, and bought a galaxy s23 ultra. It was a nice, solidly built phone, and felt like a premium device. But..
Yes, there are a ton of ways to configure the phone, and I don’t care. I’m tech savvy, but I am also busy, and I don’t really have the time to mess with all the various options. I liked the fact you can install different theme, but tbh a lot of them seemed to be made from AI graphics which looked like crap up close actually I’m the device. 
I also had the galaxy watch 5, and the integration between the two was poor. 

The S23 was the Samsung flagship at them time, and I was surprised how slow it felt, with occasional hiccups or temporary lock ups. Considering it was Samsung too device I was surprised by its apparent lack of power. Also, speaker sound quality was not great, and also not loud. The display was great, and I liked the stylus. 

I finally decided to go back to Apple with the 15 pro max, and I love it. It does what I want and need it to do, simple. And I love the tight integration the phone has with my Macs and iPads, I have several. I like being Apple to copy and paste between them, for me it’s a productivity booster. 

I can’t say whether the display is better or worse, it’s definitely good, and good enough for me. But it seems to be a MUCH more powerful device, which can’t think of a single lurch or hiccup that I’ve had, and it was pretty regular on the s23 ultra. The speakers seem to be much better, fuller, richer and louder. Imo no comparison. And the iPhone works much better with the Apple Watch than the Samsung pair did. Just not even close. 

One thing that gets me, though, is the users. So many places with comment sections that cover tech, it’s like Android users have some freaky compulsion to insult Apple products and I don’t get it. Don’t want it? Don’t flipping buy it, problem f’n solved. I don’t hate Samsung, I’m not gonna say their products are shit, they aren’t. But the iPhone works better for me, so that’s what I use. I sold my s23 to a friend, she loves it and I’m happy for her. I don’t feel the need to insult other groups because of people based on what kind of stupid phone they use.

As an app developer, I can tell you the reasons we focus on iPhone more is because Apple has forced our hand. 

1) You cannot compile your app code to make an iOS on anything other than a mac. As a result, I begrudgingly have to use a mac for work. Let that sink in. To make an iOS app, you have to buy a ~£/$1000 machine. 

2) As a result of (1) you tend to use the iPhone simulator that comes with xcode because, surprise surprise, Android studios phone simulators seem to run miraculously unusual on macOS. Yet on my personal linux machine my android studio simulators run butter smooth which hardware wise is significantly weaker than the mac I've been supplied with. So you're kinda stuck using the iPhone simulator to meet deadlines and avoid toolchain hell.


3) Publishing an app. It's a big process getting an app update published to the Apple store specifically. With Android it's a breeze. Apple requires some text for what's new in the new update; this point I kind of respect as I'm the kinda guy that reads changelogs but it's forced, so people end up putting some generic stuff, which, as an end user, I feel sucks more. You also have to tell Apple what security/encryption standards your update uses - everytime! And then if all is well you'll maybe get your new update approved in 2 to 3 days. Google however, tend to have it reviewed in a couple hours and it can all be automated, no needless stuff is made mandatory. The iOS and android version of the app I work on are identical (Flutter codebase), as mentioned above, Apple has less apps to review. And I don't believe the delay in approval to be that google automates reviews with machines as the odd occasion (once a year) a Google review might take 4 days. It's also not higher standards, Apple has never rejected an update in 3 years of working on this app but Google has flagged many useful things.

Hopefully that shines some light on the issue.

I've historically been an Apple hater, but realistically both of them are good. Does it really matter if the Pixel or Samsung loads a webpage .001 seconds faster than the iPhone? As far as operating system goes, anyone from either side trying to delude themselves into thinking the "opposing" operating system is trash is delusional. They are both well put together systems. Basically just buy whichever you want to buy and don't delude yourself into thinking those who don't use the same device are worse than you. Its just a phone at the end of the day.

In 2008, I went to a T-Mobile store with the intention of purchasing the iPhone 3G. However, upon arriving at the store, I encountered a newly released phone on display—the HTC G1. This device featured a tracking ball, touchscreen, and a sliding screen for a full keyboard. I was captivated by its features and opted to buy it instead of the iPhone. The HTC G1 had been available for some time before the concept of Android gained widespread recognition, and even the iPhone hadn't achieved the ubiquity it enjoys today. I remained loyal to Android until 2016 when I switched to an iPhone. My decision was influenced by a reduced interest in extra features and the convenience of aligning with my family, who all used iPhones. I appreciate both operating systems and believe there's something suitable for everyone. There's no need to stress over which is superior overall; what matters is what works best for you.

First off, you didn't even mention sideloading apps. That feature alone pushes the Apps category over to android. Don't like the YouTube app? There's countless variations, including some that unlock most of the premium features, for free on the internet to download the .apk file and install, completely bypassing the Google Play store and the Samsung Galaxy store. Then there's the emulators that are still on the Google Play store. Apple never lets emulators on their store for very long, if they can slip past their approval process. You can't tell me Apple wins Apps when they restrict what you can do with the hardware you bought so tightly.

Also, Samsung's ecosystem has the unique functionality of crossing over to play nicely with Google's ecosystem and Microsoft's ecosystem. The combination of those three ecosystems that all work together, as well as the variety an choice they provide, beats Apple's hands down. Don't forget about all of Google's smart home devices and features. If a smart home device can't pair with Google Home, it's dead in the water.

As for updates/support, sure, Apple has great customer service, but they'd rather sell you a new phone than fix your existing one. If we consider repairability, Apple has a long way to go. These vary widely on Android depending which company you look at. On one hand, the fair phone is a prime example of repairability being the first priority, and on the other, Samsung and Google support their old phones for way longer than I've ever had the same phone for, so in the end, there's nothing special about receiving updates for a long time. A lot of companies do that. This category should've at least been a tie, if not an android win because of the repairability of some brands.

Those 3 corrections make the winner Android 5.5 to 1.5. The only thing Apple has going for it is ease of use, but even that is iffy. To me, who has only owned Windows and Android devices, Apple devices are WAY harder for me to use. I would need a long time to acclimate to all the strange little differences in daily tasks. For someone who's never seen a smart phone before, yes, Apple is more beginner friendly, but for anyone with an ounce of tech savvy, they're both super easy to use. Apple just also has a low ceiling, meaning once you've got the basics, there's nothing deeper to dive into for more unique use cases. 

In the end, this video just reminded me why I will never buy an iPhone. I was getting tempted by the 15, but the only good thing about iPhones is the dumbed down user experience, which is a bad thing, to me. In the end, pitting one company with their own standard against an army of companies building on a shared standard is just no contest. If you limit Android to just Samsung or just Google, maybe you'd have a close fight, but if you're talking about all of Android, there's just too much variety to compare to one company, even if that company is as big as Apple.
